what_it_does: This file is a stdlib-only shared Python core that gates service startup against live GPU VRAM (via nvidia-smi) and live system RAM (via /proc/meminfo), budgets per-service GPU memory from config reservations, learned telemetry, or registry estimates, plans eviction of lower-priority running GPU services, and provides orphan-safe teardown for both systemd units and manual process groups.
observations: Imports only stdlib plus sibling modules registry, systemd, and telemetry so it can run in a minimal 3.14 bridge environment.
Named gpu.py deliberately because resource would shadow the stdlib resource module.
VRAM budget priority is config.gpu_util × ceiling first, then learned_vram telemetry, then static registry vram_mb.
Running detection for managed services uses unit state, not ports, because model services share ports such as :8000.
Includes both GPU-VRAM and system-RAM capacity legs: check_fit_unified, ram_fit, and validate_combo_capacity.
Config-time validate_combo_capacity rejects loadouts whose estimated totals exceed hardware ceilings before runtime.
Eviction order is hardcoded models → brain → voice, largest-first within each group.
Teardown uses systemctl stop for units to kill the cgroup and reap vLLM EngineCore, and process-group SIGTERM/SIGKILL for manual services.
State formatting labels each running service's VRAM source as reserved, measured, or est.
Falls back to estimate-based capacity math when nvidia-smi or /proc/meminfo is unavailable rather than blocking.
standouts: Unified GPU-VRAM + system-RAM capacity decision surface with live measurement and config-time validation.
Orphan-safe teardown that distinguishes systemd cgroup stops from manual process-group stops.
VRAM provenance labels (reserved/measured/est) exposed in operator-facing state output.
Per-unit running detection to avoid false positives from shared model service ports.
conventions: Heavy docstrings include dates, policy owners, and incident rationale (e.g., 2026-06-06, 2026-06-28 cascade).
Public API aliased explicitly (budget_of = budget_vram) for cross-module consumers.
Private helpers prefixed with underscore; public functions listed in module docstring.
Registry-is-truth for configurable defaults (ram_headroom_mb) with a local fallback constant.
Graceful degradation: missing measurement tools return None and callers fall back to estimates with present/warn flags.
Uses subprocess and /proc rather than external libraries for GPU/RAM readings.
concerns: NVSMI path is hardcoded to /usr/lib/wsl/lib/nvidia-smi, making the module WSL-specific unless PATH fallback succeeds.
check_fit's fallback (ceiling - committed estimates) can be wrong when non-Company processes hold GPU memory.
plan_eviction may return projected_free < need when all candidates are exhausted; caller must check but this is easy to miss.
Manual teardown relies on pgrep -f against a script token, which can match unrelated processes with similar command lines.
No multi-GPU awareness; all figures assume a single card.
The --force override mentioned in the policy docstring is not implemented inside this file; it must be handled by callers.
notes: A WSL-aware, stdlib-only capacity gate that treats GPU and RAM as live, shared resources and stops services by cgroup to avoid orphan processes.
questions: Where is the --force override implemented and how does it interact with check_fit?
What are the exact contracts of registry, systemd, and telemetry modules (e.g., ceiling_mb, learned_vram, is_active)?
How is the service dictionary structured (required keys for manage, config, group, port)?
How does learned_vram distinguish stale telemetry from a previous config.gpu_util?
What unit names or port mappings does the systemd module use?
Is there any handling for multiple GPUs or GPU index selection?
What is the '3.14 bridge' environment this module targets?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches and extends the stated purpose: it is indeed the shared VRAM authority and orphan-safe teardown core, but it also adds system-RAM legs and config-time combo validation beyond the original GPU-only description.
apparent_themes: Live measurement over static estimates, with honest fallback labeling.
Actuation gating to prevent resource blowups before they happen.
Shared core to avoid duplicate budget/teardown logic across CLI and voice stack.
intention_signals: The file is trying to make resource-driven service starts deterministic and safe on a single-GPU WSL box: measure truth, budget honestly, refuse or evict before overcommit, and stop things cleanly enough that vLLM EngineCore cannot escape as an orphan.
novelty: The combination of config-time combo validation, live dual-resource (VRAM + RAM) gating, source-labeled state output, and cgroup-aware orphan-safe teardown in one stdlib-only shared core is unusual for a small CLI module.
contribution: It contributes a reusable, measurement-first capacity gate and a safe teardown primitive that any fused system would keep to avoid GPU/RAM overcommit and orphaned model processes.
summary_for_embedding: company/ops/cli/gpu.py is a stdlib-only shared core that gates service startup against live GPU VRAM and system RAM, budgets per-service GPU memory, plans priority eviction, and performs orphan-safe teardown via systemd cgroups or process groups.
intention_for_embedding: It was built to be the single resource authority for both the company CLI and the voice lifecycle, preventing overcommit and orphaned vLLM processes on a WSL GPU host.

symbols.does: Returns whether a service occupies the GPU by checking its registry VRAM estimate or config gpu_util flag.
symbols.description: A simple predicate used to filter the registry for GPU-holding services; it treats either an explicit config.gpu_util or any registry vram_mb estimate as evidence that the service pins GPU memory.

symbols.does: Computes the VRAM budget for a service, preferring config.gpu_util slice, then learned telemetry, then the registry estimate.
symbols.description: Implements a three-tier priority: for config-driven model services the reservation is config.gpu_util × ceiling (treated as authoritative because vLLM pins that slice), otherwise it falls back to measured telemetry and finally the static registry vram_mb.

symbols.does: Labels the origin of a service's VRAM figure as reserved, measured, or estimated.
symbols.description: Mirrors budget_vram's priority to expose provenance in state output, making it explicit whether the displayed number is a config reservation, a telemetry-derived load delta, or an unmeasured registry guess.

symbols.does: Checks whether a service is currently running using unit state for managed services and port openness for manual services.
symbols.description: Avoids port checks for managed services because model services share ports (e.g., chat services on :8000), which would falsely mark siblings as active; manual services have no unit and fall back to their unique port.

symbols.does: Resolves the nvidia-smi executable path, preferring a WSL path and falling back to PATH.
symbols.description: First checks the hardcoded WSL location /usr/lib/wsl/lib/nvidia-smi, then uses shutil.which to locate nvidia-smi on the host PATH.

symbols.does: Reads current GPU memory and utilization from nvidia-smi and returns them in MB/percent.
symbols.description: Queries memory.used, memory.free, memory.total, and utilization.gpu via nvidia-smi CSV output; returns None if the tool is missing or fails.

symbols.does: Lists running GPU-occupying services with their budgeted VRAM.
symbols.description: Iterates the registry, filters services that are GPU services and currently running, and returns (key, vram_mb) pairs using budget_vram.

symbols.does: Sums the budgeted VRAM of all currently running GPU services.
symbols.description: A convenience aggregate used in fallback capacity math and state display; it relies on running_gpu_services and therefore on the same budget_vram estimates.

symbols.does: Formats a human-readable snapshot of GPU, RAM, and running GPU services.
symbols.description: Reads live GPU and system RAM, then lists running GPU services with their budget and provenance label; intended to be shown on every up and on refusal so operators see what holds the card.

symbols.does: Decides whether starting a set of services fits into measured free GPU VRAM.
symbols.description: Sums budget_vram only for not-yet-running GPU services in the requested set, compares against live nvidia-smi free memory, and falls back to ceiling-minus-committed estimates when measurement is unavailable.

symbols.does: Reports whether a selected set of GPU services fits the card ceiling and fits measured free VRAM now.
symbols.description: Answers two distinct questions: capacity against the card ceiling independent of current load, and live fit against measured free memory; when the live fit fails it names candidate evictions and projected free memory.

symbols.does: Selects running GPU services to stop in order to make room for a requested set.
symbols.description: Evicts by group priority (models, then brain, then voice) and largest-first within a group, sparing the requested set, until projected free memory meets the need or candidates run out.

symbols.does: Extracts the identifying script path from a manual service's run command for pgrep.
symbols.description: Scans the run string for tokens ending in .py or .sh, expands ~, and returns the path; used to target manual process groups during teardown.

symbols.does: Stops a service in an orphan-safe way, using systemd for units and process groups for manual services.
symbols.description: For unit-managed services it delegates to systemctl stop to kill the whole cgroup, reaping vLLM EngineCore and spawn helpers; for manual services it SIGTERMs the process group and SIGKILLs survivors after a timeout.
